headline: Tech Weekly: Tesla's surge, robot footballers target glory
summary: STORY: From Tesla driving up sales to robot footballers competing in their own competition...This is Tech Weekly.:: Tech WeeklyTesla beat Wall Street estimates when it posted record second-quarter deliveries.The electric vehicle maker delivered more than 480,000 vehicles between April and June - up about a quarter from a year ago.The results were partly driven by a rebound in Europe.China was another bright spot, although demand was still under pressure in the U.S.The World Cup witnessed a technological first during Norway's 2-1 win over Brazil in the Round of 16. An advanced humanoid robot called Atlas delivered the match ball to the referee at half-time.The robot was developed by U.S. firm Boston Dynamics and presented by tournament sponsor Hyundai Motor.Atlas can impersonate famous goal celebrations, including Norway striker Erling Haaland's meditation pose.While Atlas delivered a ball, robots in South Korea showed they could actually play soccer.They competed at the finals of RoboCup 2026 in Incheon - a major robotics and AI competition.Organisers said more than 3,000 competitors from 45 countries took part, and many teams reportedly used platforms developed by China's Booster Robotics.The robots stumbled around and collided with rivals but got back up to keep playing, with Team Hephaestus of Tsinghua University winning the contest.Google lost its long fight against a record $4 billion plus fine imposed by the EU eight years ago.The European Commission had punished the tech giant for its dealings with phone manufacturers.The phone makers were forced to pre-install Google Search, the Chrome browser and the Google Play app store on their Android devices - and prevented from using rival systems.A Google spokesperson said the judgment didn't account for its efforts to make sure Android is open, interoperable and free.And customers at the Lounge X coffee shop in Seoul are now assisted by a robot named Baris.Its a technological sight which has become more common across South Korea.Lounge X CEO Kim Dongjin said the firm turned to robots and self-service to overcome rising labor costs.Kim says his company runs eight such unstaffed 24-hour coffee shops across South Korea, while four stores are still operated by humans.

raw.headline: Tech Weekly: Tesla's surge, robot footballers target glory
raw.summary: STORY: From Tesla driving up sales to robot footballers competing in their own competition...This is Tech Weekly.:: Tech WeeklyTesla beat Wall Street estimates when it posted record second-quarter deliveries.The electric vehicle maker delivered more than 480,000 vehicles between April and June - up about a quarter from a year ago.The results were partly driven by a rebound in Europe.China was another bright spot, although demand was still under pressure in the U.S.The World Cup witnessed a technological first during Norway's 2-1 win over Brazil in the Round of 16. An advanced humanoid robot called Atlas delivered the match ball to the referee at half-time.The robot was developed by U.S. firm Boston Dynamics and presented by tournament sponsor Hyundai Motor.Atlas can impersonate famous goal celebrations, including Norway striker Erling Haaland's meditation pose.While Atlas delivered a ball, robots in South Korea showed they could actually play soccer.They competed at the finals of RoboCup 2026 in Incheon - a major robotics and AI competition.Organisers said more than 3,000 competitors from 45 countries took part, and many teams reportedly used platforms developed by China's Booster Robotics.The robots stumbled around and collided with rivals but got back up to keep playing, with Team Hephaestus of Tsinghua University winning the contest.Google lost its long fight against a record $4 billion plus fine imposed by the EU eight years ago.The European Commission had punished the tech giant for its dealings with phone manufacturers.The phone makers were forced to pre-install Google Search, the Chrome browser and the Google Play app store on their Android devices - and prevented from using rival systems.A Google spokesperson said the judgment didn't account for its efforts to make sure Android is open, interoperable and free.And customers at the Lounge X coffee shop in Seoul are now assisted by a robot named Baris.Its a technological sight which has become more common across South Korea.Lounge X CEO Kim Dongjin said the firm turned to robots and self-service to overcome rising labor costs.Kim says his company runs eight such unstaffed 24-hour coffee shops across South Korea, while four stores are still operated by humans.
